title: Social Integration and Academic Divergence: International Student Experiences at HKUST(GZ)

social_experience: Relatively easy to make friends due to shared Central Asian background and similar circumstances.
Feels a general sense of social belonging; no strong exclusion experiences.
Social circle mostly from same background or country.
Shared language within the group reduces friction and eases communication.
interaction_with_chinese_students: Interactions are interesting but involve language friction; can also be humorous.
Shared freshman experience is a connecting point across groups.
Difference in high school cultures (Chinese students had far less free time) creates some distance.
Language proficiency — not language difference itself — is identified as the key barrier.
Cultural differences seen as interesting rather than problematic.
Students mostly stay in separate groups; partly attributed to the university still being young.
academic_impact: Social experience has had a positive effect: peers share opportunities and useful information.
Strong social networks help with academic choices (e.g., knowing which professors to select).
Does not feel academically disadvantaged from social factors.
Performs better in UFUG than UCUG — not naturally strong at presentations.
Has not had enough UCUG courses yet to fully compare.
barriers_and_recommendations: University lecture culture (attend and leave without speaking) reduces organic socialisation.
Appreciates events organised by the Global Affairs department for cross-year mixing.
No specific recommendations; hopes the university develops ideas that encourage broader dialogue.

background: Central Asian, Year 1, currently in second semester at HKUST(GZ).
social_experience: First impression was exciting (clubs, people, opportunities); expectations adjusted once exams began.
Personally easy to make friends — outgoing personality and willingness to initiate.
Does not feel belonging: clubs and events conducted in Chinese despite English advertising.
Mostly spends time with Central Asian peers; some Chinese/Hong Kong friends from sport and study.
Clear exclusion example: attended Robotics Club but was sidelined by Chinese-only conversation among seniors.
interaction_with_chinese_students: Generally positive but shallow — no deep personal connections formed.
Easier when Chinese students are outgoing and take initiative.
Harder when no effort comes from the Chinese student's side.
Does not consider language the main barrier (English environment should apply); behavioral difference is more prominent.
Notes Chinese students may have had less open social experience in high school.
Students stay in separate groups — driven by shared language and shared circumstances.
academic_impact: Keeps social and academic life clearly separated; friendships do not directly affect grades.
Social connections help access useful information (RC office, admissions) shared informally in English.
Prefers to work with own-background friends in group assignments (e.g., worked with Ramazan in biology).
Does not feel academically disadvantaged socially.
Social skills matter more in UCUG courses; UFUG is more exam-focused and less dependent on social ties.
International students tend to outperform in UCUG; Chinese in UFUG (preparation-driven difference).
barriers_and_recommendations: Main barrier: willingness — integration must be two-sided, not driven by internationals alone.
University falls short: Chinese-only emails, posters, and subject lines exclude international students.
Key recommendation: appoint a dedicated international student representative to channel concerns directly to university administration.
Highlights gap between polished social media portrayal and the real difficulties international students face.

background: Kazakhstani (ethnically Uzbek), Year 1, Advanced Materials. First time living abroad.
social_experience: Found many friendly people upon arrival; positive initial social impression.
Does not feel belonging — clubs are conducted entirely in Chinese, excluding international students.
Spends most time with students from own background.
Some mild disconnection in events; partly attributes this to himself rather than the environment.
interaction_with_chinese_students: Limited but not impossible — some Chinese students are willing to connect.
English proficiency is the key differentiating factor for connection.
Language is the single most important factor in social relationships.
Cultural differences are manageable; has adapted reasonably well.
Students predominantly stay in separate groups.
academic_impact: Limited direct effect on academics, but acknowledges potential benefit if social ties were stronger.
Excluded from Chinese WeChat study groups — misses informal knowledge and resource sharing.
Group projects difficult as the only international student: Chinese peers continue speaking Chinese despite requests to switch.
UCUG courses much easier than UFUG for international students; opposite pattern observed for Chinese students.
Chinese peer networks provide an academic information advantage not available to internationals.
barriers_and_recommendations: Language barrier is primary; but notes that openness and attitude matter even if language were resolved.
Clubs should be conducted in English or at minimum bilingually.
More campus events in English needed — almost half of current events are Chinese-only.
Overlooked issue: Chinese students are genuinely surprised by international students' UFUG struggles — expectation gap goes unaddressed.

background: Kazakhstani, Year 1 AI student; arrived with fellow Kazakhstani students.
social_experience: Had a familiar social base on arrival; gradually felt lonely mid-first semester.
Uncertain about belonging — gap between himself and the high-achieving Chinese academic environment.
Mostly spends time with Central Asian peers.
Was excluded from small friend-group circles early on; situation improved over time.
interaction_with_chinese_students: Only knows a small number of Chinese students; main contact is his roommate.
Most Chinese students are introverted and stay within their own groups.
Core barrier: Chinese students lack confidence to communicate in English — not ability.
Unlikely to form many meaningful Chinese friendships; does not see the environment as receptive.
Language is the most critical barrier, compounded by introversion.
academic_impact: Clear negative impact: studied alone throughout the year with no peer support network.
Emotional toll of isolation affected motivation (improved over time).
No access to study groups — even within the Kazakhstani community.
International group dynamics helped in presentation-based courses (sociology example).
Gaokao-prepared Chinese students have a structural academic advantage in content-heavy courses.
Social networks helpful in UCUG courses; did not compensate for gaps in UFUG.
UFUG harder due to weaker high school content preparation.
barriers_and_recommendations: Barriers: language + university's failure to actively promote integration.
University does the bare minimum; friendships formed through personal initiative only.
Recommends: structured university programmes to facilitate both integration and study practices.
Overlooked issue: international students were not academically prepared the same way as Chinese students — this goes unacknowledged.

respondents.name: Younger
extracurriculars: National silver in English speech competition

social_environment_and_perspective: Describes HKUST(GZ) as small, friendly, and socially low-pressure compared to larger campuses.
Personally very open and culturally curious; knows most international students on campus.
Has formed deep connections with some international students through joint activities.
interaction_with_international_students: Estimates ~70% of Chinese students find forming deep cross-cultural friendships difficult.
Language is the biggest driver of separate social circles — speaking only Chinese excludes non-speakers.
Main obstacle: Chinese students' lack of English-speaking confidence, not ability.
Barrier is thin — one greeting is often enough to start a genuine connection.
Has seen introverted students with limited English successfully push themselves to connect.
Does not personally hold the bias of befriending only high academic achievers.
academic_impact: Social integration enriches communication-based academic work (public speaking, presentations).
Cross-cultural background can produce distinctively strong academic perspectives.
Better in UCUG (Intercultural Communication) — collaborated effectively with international students.
Weaker in UFUG (C++) — individual technical ability matters more than communication.
International students outperform Chinese in communication-heavy courses; Chinese outperform in STEM due to prior high school coverage.
When content is genuinely new to both groups, performance differences are minimal.
Active vs. passive group participation is individual, not strictly Chinese vs. international.
Reverses the social-network-performance link: high performers attract strong networks, not the other way around.
barriers_and_recommendations: Biggest barrier: lack of shared experiences — Chinese students self-sort into Chinese-only groups.
University provides a platform but does not actively connect different student communities.
RC activities follow only Chinese cultural calendar — no acknowledgment of international occasions (e.g., Kazakh New Year).
Key recommendation: host cultural events around international occasions (e.g., Kazakh New Year banquet) to create shared experiences and introduce Chinese students to other cultures.
Overlooked issue: Chinese students often fail to notice international students' cultural moments — a quiet but real form of exclusion.

common_barriers: Language is the single biggest barrier — both proficiency and speaking confidence.
Chinese students' lack of English-speaking confidence is more problematic than actual English ability.
Pre-existing social groups and self-sorting by language limit natural cross-cultural mixing.
Cultural and behavioral differences (high school experience, communication norms) add secondary friction.
social_trends: All respondents confirm students mostly form separate circles — international vs. Chinese.
International students primarily form bonds with peers from their own country or region.
The few outgoing, English-confident Chinese students are consistently cited as easiest to connect with.
Even within the international community, sub-groups form along national lines.
International students commonly report partial or no sense of belonging.
academic_insights: Social networks help with logistics — information sharing, group work, course selection — but direct effect on grades is debated.
Exclusion from Chinese WeChat study groups is a concrete, recurring academic disadvantage.
International students consistently perform better in UCUG/communication-heavy courses; Chinese students in UFUG/content-heavy courses.
The UCUG/UFUG performance gap is attributed to different academic preparation systems, not raw ability.
Group project dynamics disadvantage international students in Chinese-majority groups where Chinese is spoken exclusively.
university_role: Near-universal agreement that the university does not do enough to support integration.
Campus infrastructure (clubs, events, emails, posters) is predominantly Chinese-language.
No recognition of international cultural occasions by the Residential College.
More intercultural activities and events conducted in English.
Bilingual communications, emails, and campus posters.
Structured integration programmes with clear outcomes.
Appointment of an international student representative.
Cultural celebration events that include non-Chinese traditions.
The Chinese respondent shares this critical view — providing cross-perspective validation of international student concerns.
